title: Coffee Ratio Calculator – Water to Coffee
shortDescription: Find the perfect water-to-coffee ratio for pour-over and drip.
description: Enter your coffee dose and a golden ratio to get the exact amount of water. Everything runs in your browser — no signup.
metaDescription: Calculate the ideal water-to-coffee ratio for pour-over and drip coffee. Enter your coffee dose and a golden ratio from 1:15 to 1:18 to get exact water in grams.

result.label: Water needed
result.empty: Enter your coffee weight to see the water amount.
result.note: {coffee} g of coffee at a {ratio} ratio.

sections.heading: What is the coffee-to-water ratio?
paragraphs: The brew ratio is the proportion of ground coffee to water, written as 1:N. A 1:16 ratio means 16 grams of water for every 1 gram of coffee. The 'golden ratio' for filter coffee sits between 1:15 and 1:18 — lower numbers give a stronger, heavier cup and higher numbers a lighter, more delicate one.

sections.heading: How to use this calculator
paragraphs: Weigh your coffee in grams, pick a ratio, and the calculator multiplies the two to give the water in grams. Because 1 gram of water is almost exactly 1 millilitre, the same number works as millilitres. Start at 1:16 and adjust to taste on your next brew.

howTo.name: Weigh your coffee
howTo.text: Enter the ground-coffee dose in grams (e.g. 18 g).

howTo.name: Choose a ratio
howTo.text: Pick a golden ratio between 1:15 and 1:18.

howTo.name: Read the water
howTo.text: The result shows the water to use, in grams and millilitres.

faq.q: What is the best coffee-to-water ratio?
faq.a: 1:16 is a reliable starting point for filter coffee. Go to 1:15 for a stronger cup or 1:18 for a lighter one.

faq.q: Is 1 gram of water the same as 1 ml?
faq.a: Yes, near room temperature 1 g of water equals about 1 ml, so you can use the result as either grams or millilitres.

faq.q: What ratio should I use for espresso?
faq.a: Espresso uses much tighter ratios (about 1:2). This calculator targets pour-over and drip brewing.
A scale gives the most consistent results, but you can approximate: a level tablespoon of ground coffee is roughly 5 g.

faq.q: Why does my coffee taste bitter or sour?
faq.a: Bitterness often means too little water (over-extraction); sourness means too much water or too coarse a grind. Adjust the ratio and grind size.
